election.name: French Municipal Elections 2026
election.description: Municipal elections across all French communes. Voters elect municipal councils, which then elect the mayor. Cities over 1,000 inhabitants use proportional list voting over two rounds. A new law for 2026 abolishes arrondissement-level councils in Paris, Lyon and Marseille, replacing them with a single city-wide list.

PS.name: Parti Socialiste
PS.description: The traditional party of the French left, in power under Mitterrand (1981–95) and Hollande (2012–17). Now the backbone of the NFP left coalition. Champions public services, workers' rights and European integration. Has lost ground to both the radical left (LFI) and Macron's centrists since 2017.

NFP.name: Nouveau Front Populaire
NFP.description: A left-wing electoral coalition formed for the 2024 legislative elections, named after the 1930s Popular Front. Includes PS, EELV, PCF, Place Publique and LFI. Won the most seats in 2024 but couldn't form a majority government. The coalition's internal tensions — particularly between PS and LFI — remain a defining feature of French left politics.

EELV.name: Europe Écologie Les Verts
EELV.description: France's Green party, which rode a 'green wave' in the 2020 municipal elections to capture Lyon, Bordeaux, Strasbourg and Grenoble. Focuses on urban ecology, cycling infrastructure, environmental policy and social justice. Governs in coalition with PS and PCF in most cities it controls.

LR.name: Les Républicains
LR.description: The successor to the UMP and RPR, France's traditional Gaullist conservative party. Once the dominant force of the French right, LR has been hollowed out since Macron's 2017 breakthrough — losing members to both Renaissance and the hard right. Champions lower taxes, law and order, stricter immigration and European reform.

Renaissance.name: Renaissance / Ensemble
Renaissance.description: Emmanuel Macron's party, founded as 'En Marche' in 2016. Explicitly anti-left/right, it combines economic liberalism (labour market reform, pro-business) with social progressivism. Has struggled to build local roots and in 2026 largely chose to back better-implanted candidates from other parties rather than run its own.

RN.name: Rassemblement National
RN.description: Founded as the Front National by Jean-Marie Le Pen in 1972, renamed in 2018 under Marine Le Pen's leadership. France's largest party by first-round votes in recent elections. Anti-immigration, Eurosceptic, economically protectionist with a welfare-state tinge. Its 2026 municipal push — if successful in Marseille — would give it its largest city in France.

LFI.name: La France Insoumise
LFI.description: Founded by Jean-Luc Mélenchon in 2016, LFI is France's most successful hard-left force in decades. Supports a 6th Republic, increased minimum wage, wealth taxes and a break from NATO. Often runs separately from PS and EELV, which tensions the NFP coalition. Strong in urban areas with large working-class and young populations.

PCF.name: Parti Communiste Français
PCF.description: One of the oldest parties in France, the PCF was a major political force from the Liberation to the 1980s. Now much reduced, it remains influential in municipal politics through its network of elected officials and typically allies with PS and EELV.

UDR.name: Union des Droites pour la République
UDR.description: Formed in 2024 by LR president Éric Ciotti after he controversially allied LR with RN, triggering a party split. UDR sits between traditional conservatism and RN-style national populism. Allied with RN in 2024 legislative elections.

Reconquête.name: Reconquête
Reconquête.description: Founded by polemicist Éric Zemmour in 2021. Even further right than RN on immigration and Islam, Reconquête is culturally ultra-conservative and explicitly anti-Islam. Struggled to build an electoral base beyond its media profile.

Horizons.name: Horizons
Horizons.description: Founded by former Prime Minister Édouard Philippe in 2021, Horizons is part of the Macron coalition but with a more centre-right flavour. Philippe is seen as a likely presidential candidate in 2027. Strong local roots in Le Havre and other cities.

DVG.name: Divers Gauche
DVG.description: A catch-all category for centre-left politicians not formally affiliated with a major party, often local figures with pragmatic records.

DVD.name: Divers Droite
DVD.description: Centre-right independents. Many French mayors — especially in smaller communes — stand under this label, prioritising local governance over national party identity.

major_races.name: Paris
major_races.context: Paris has been governed by the left since 2001. Anne Hidalgo, who served two terms (2014–2026), announced in November 2024 she would not seek a third term — designating Emmanuel Grégoire, her long-serving first deputy, as the left's candidate. The 2026 election introduces a major institutional change: arrondissement-level councils are abolished, with the entire city voting on a single list, making the race more decisive than any in living memory. The leading battle is between Grégoire and Rachida Dati, Sarkozy's former justice minister who has been mayor of the 7th arrondissement since 2008. Macron's camp backs Bournazel but Renaissance has no candidate of its own.

macron_endorsement.note: Renaissance officially backs Bournazel (Horizons). Former ministers Clément Beaune and Marlène Schiappa are on his list. Macron himself has not publicly intervened.

candidates.name: Emmanuel Grégoire
candidates.note: Former first deputy to Hidalgo. Coalition includes PS, EELV, PCF, Place Publique — but NOT LFI.

candidates.name: Rachida Dati
candidates.note: Mayor of the 7th arrondissement. Faces corruption trial in September 2026. Some informal Renaissance support at individual level.

candidates.name: Pierre-Yves Bournazel
candidates.note: Macron's official pick. Ex-ministers on his list.

candidates.name: Sophia Chikirou
candidates.note: MP and Mélenchon ally. Running separately from the PS-led left.

candidates.name: Thierry Mariani
candidates.note: MEP. RN candidate for Paris.

candidates.name: Sarah Knafo
candidates.note: Polling at ~11.5%. Runoff stance will be decisive for alliances.

polls.methodology: Online panel, n=1,200 registered voters, Paris only
polls.note: Five candidates at or near 10% threshold. Runoff alliances — particularly where Knafo and Bournazel voters go — will be decisive.

major_races.name: Marseille
major_races.context: France's second city has a turbulent political history. The right controlled city hall for 25 years under Jean-Claude Gaudin (1995–2020) before a fragile left-green coalition won in 2020 under Michèle Rubirola, who later handed power to Benoît Payan. Marseille has some of France's highest poverty rates, a chronic housing crisis and persistent drug-related violence — issues that have handed the RN a powerful platform. Franck Allisio, RN MP personally backed by Marine Le Pen, is neck and neck with Payan in polls. The burning question: will Marseille become the largest RN-governed city in France? A Payan–Allisio runoff would be the election's highest-stakes contest.

macron_endorsement.note: Renaissance officially backs Vassal (LR), who secured a joint LR-Renaissance list. Vassal is a pragmatic local figure — not ideologically Macronist, but the centrist camp has no viable alternative.

candidates.name: Benoît Payan
candidates.note: Left coalition: PS, PCF, Écologistes, Génération.s, Place Publique, Parti Animaliste.

candidates.name: Franck Allisio
candidates.note: RN MP for the 12th constituency. Marine Le Pen's personal pick. Polling within 3 points of Payan.

candidates.name: Martine Vassal
candidates.note: President of the Aix-Marseille Métropole and Bouches-du-Rhône department. Joint LR-Renaissance list. Lost 2020 narrowly.

candidates.name: Sébastien Delogu
candidates.note: LFI deputy for the northern constituencies. Refused alliance with Payan. Backed by Mélenchon who visited March 7.

polls.methodology: Phone + online, n=1,000 registered voters, Marseille
polls.note: If Vassal backs Allisio in round 2 (as he has called for), the runoff becomes a toss-up. Payan has categorically refused alliance with LFI's Delogu.

major_races.name: Lyon
major_races.context: The 2026 Lyon race is one of the election's biggest potential upsets. Grégory Doucet won in 2020 as part of the 'green wave' that also swept Bordeaux and Strasbourg. His term has been marked by ambitious cycling policy, restrictions on cars in the city centre and a ban on certain events in public parks — popular with some residents, deeply controversial with others. His challenger is Jean-Michel Aulas, the legendary president of Olympique Lyonnais for 35 years (1987–2022), who has no prior political experience but commands near-mythical status among Lyon residents. Aulas has secured endorsements from LR, Horizons, MoDem and Renaissance in an unusually broad right-centre coalition. Current polling shows him winning a hypothetical runoff 57–43 against Doucet.

macron_endorsement.note: Renaissance and Horizons both back Aulas. He is the centrist camp's clearest potential success story in 2026.

candidates.name: Grégory Doucet
candidates.note: Coalition of Greens, PS, PCF.

candidates.name: Jean-Michel Aulas
candidates.note: Former OL president. LR, Horizons, Renaissance, MoDem coalition. No prior political experience.

candidates.name: Anaïs Belouassa-Cherifi
candidates.note: MP for Lyon 1st constituency, elected 2024.

candidates.name: Alexandre Dupalais
candidates.note: Joint RN–UDR list.

candidates.name: Georges Képénékian
candidates.note: Former acting mayor (2017-2020) and Gérard Collomb's former first deputy. Running as independent centre-left.

polls.methodology: Online panel, n=1,000 registered voters, Lyon
polls.note: A major potential upset. Aulas is favoured in a runoff. LFI's strong showing complicates left-wing fusion strategy.

major_races.name: Toulouse
major_races.context: The 'Pink City' (named for its rose brick architecture, not its politics) has been governed by the right under Jean-Luc Moudenc since 2014, after a brief Socialist interlude. With Airbus, a booming aerospace and tech sector and France's fastest-growing population among major cities, Toulouse's prosperity has been a calling card for Moudenc. The left is unusually united behind François Briançon (PS-led Gauche Unie), polling at 30% and within striking distance. However, LFI's François Piquemal — a sitting MP — is running separately at 23%, threatening to split the left vote and help Moudenc through.

macron_endorsement.note: Renaissance backs incumbent Moudenc, who is seen as a reliable pro-business, centre-right manager. No separate Renaissance list.

candidates.name: Jean-Luc Moudenc
candidates.note: Seeks 3rd term. Pro-business, pro-Airbus platform. Renaissance backing.

candidates.name: François Briançon
candidates.note: PS, EELV, PCF, Génération.s, Place Publique coalition — but NOT LFI.

candidates.name: François Piquemal
candidates.note: MP for Toulouse 4th. Refuses to join Briançon's list. 23% makes him a potential kingmaker.

candidates.name: Julien Leonardelli
candidates.note: MEP. First serious RN push in Toulouse.

polls.methodology: Online panel, n=900 registered voters, Toulouse
polls.note: Moudenc leads but if Briançon and Piquemal fuse for round 2 the race becomes a genuine toss-up.

major_races.name: Nice
major_races.context: Nice is the election's most dramatic personal contest. Christian Estrosi has governed the city since 2008 and is a central figure in Macron's coalition (he joined Horizons). His challenger is Éric Ciotti — until recently his LR colleague, ally and fellow Alpes-Maritimes representative — who in 2024 controversially allied LR with RN, was expelled from LR, and founded UDR. Two former friends are now opponents in the city they've both dominated for decades. RN has historically polled well in the Alpes-Maritimes, and with UDR as Ciotti's vehicle, this is effectively a RN-adjacent challenge. Ciotti is currently polling ahead.

macron_endorsement.note: Estrosi is himself an Horizons figure and part of the Macron coalition. Renaissance fully backs him.

candidates.name: Christian Estrosi
candidates.list: Nice, la ville que nous aimons
candidates.note: Mayor since 2008. Horizons, the Macron coalition. Previously LR.

candidates.name: Éric Ciotti
candidates.note: Former LR president who allied with RN in 2024, triggering a party split. His UDR-RN coalition is polling ahead of Estrosi.

candidates.name: TBD
candidates.note: Left coalition candidate.

polls.methodology: Online panel, n=800 registered voters, Nice
polls.note: An extraordinary intra-right duel between two former close allies. Ciotti is favoured but left-wing votes in round 2 could save Estrosi.

major_races.name: Nantes
major_races.context: A solid PS stronghold for decades — Jean-Marc Ayrault governed for 23 years before becoming Hollande's prime minister, handing power to Johanna Rolland in 2014. Nantes has been celebrated for urban regeneration projects like the Île de Nantes and the 'Machines de l'Île' cultural installations. Rolland seeks a third term and leads in polls, but the right is united for the first time in years under Foulques Chombart de Lauwe with LR, Renaissance, Horizons and MoDem support. LFI's William Aucant runs separately. A rare city where the macronist and right-wing vote is consolidated rather than divided.

macron_endorsement.note: Renaissance backs the joint LR-Horizons-MoDem list. Sarah El Haïry (Macron's former secretary of state for youth) is Chombart de Lauwe's number two.

candidates.name: Johanna Rolland
candidates.note: PS + EELV + PCF + alliés. 3rd term bid.

candidates.name: Foulques Chombart de Lauwe
candidates.note: LR + Horizons + MoDem + Renaissance. No. 2: Sarah El Haïry (MoDem, ex-minister).

candidates.name: William Aucant
candidates.note: Regional councillor.

polls.methodology: Online panel, n=800 registered voters, Nantes
polls.note: Rolland leads but the united right is closer than in 2020. LFI fusion decisions in round 2 will matter.

major_races.name: Strasbourg
major_races.context: Strasbourg is the election's most unusual intra-left battle. Jeanne Barseghian won in 2020 as part of the green wave, becoming the city's first female and first Green mayor. But she faces an extraordinary challenger: Catherine Trautmann, the PS grande dame who was herself mayor of Strasbourg from 1989 to 2001 and a European Commissioner. At 72, Trautmann is making a comeback, polling 11 points ahead of the incumbent in some surveys. Rather than a left vs right contest, Strasbourg's 2026 election is a battle between two wings of the left — a referendum on the 'green wave' legacy.

macron_endorsement.note: Renaissance backs the Horizons candidate Jakubowicz, but with Trautmann and Barseghian dominating, the centrist vote is marginal here.

candidates.name: Jeanne Barseghian
candidates.note: First Green mayor of Strasbourg.

candidates.name: Catherine Trautmann
candidates.note: Former mayor (1989–2001) and EU Commissioner. Polling 11pts ahead of Barseghian.

polls.methodology: Online panel, n=800 registered voters, Strasbourg
polls.note: A historic intra-left contest. Trautmann, 72, is positioned to reclaim the city she governed 25 years ago.

major_races.name: Bordeaux
major_races.context: Bordeaux was a Gaullist and centre-right stronghold for over 30 years under Jacques Chaban-Delmas and then Alain Juppé (1995–2019). It was captured by the Greens in 2020 under Pierre Hurmic. His term has been polarising: celebrated by environmentalists for radical cycling measures and water conservation, but denounced by others for removing fountains, restricting public events in parks and what critics call ideological governance. Thomas Cazenave (Renaissance MP) leads the centrist challenge. A dissenting right-wing candidacy by Philippe Dessertine could split the anti-Hurmic vote.

macron_endorsement.note: Cazenave is Renaissance's own candidate here — one of the few cities where Macron's party runs its own list rather than backing an ally.

candidates.name: Pierre Hurmic
candidates.note: EELV + PS + PCF coalition.

candidates.name: Thomas Cazenave
candidates.note: MP for Bordeaux. Renaissance's direct candidate.

candidates.name: Philippe Dessertine
candidates.note: University professor and economist. 15% polling threatens to split right-wing vote.

polls.methodology: Online panel, n=800 registered voters, Bordeaux
polls.note: Dead heat in projections. Dessertine's 15% is critical: if he fuses with Cazenave for round 2, Hurmic loses. If not, Hurmic likely holds.

major_races.name: Montpellier
major_races.context: Montpellier is one of France's fastest-growing cities, driven by its universities, medical sector and Mediterranean lifestyle. It has been governed by the left since 1977. Michaël Delafosse won in 2020 with a broad left coalition and has pursued ambitious public transport expansion including free buses. Renaissance chose not to field a candidate here, reflecting the futility of the centrist vote in this reliably left city. The RN is mounting its strongest Montpellier challenge yet.

macron_endorsement.note: Renaissance chose not to field a candidate or endorse in Montpellier — an unusual absence reflecting the city's left-wing solidarity.

candidates.name: Michaël Delafosse
candidates.note: PS + EELV + PCF coalition.

candidates.name: TBD
candidates.note: RN mounting its strongest push in the city.

polls.methodology: Online panel, n=700 registered voters, Montpellier

major_races.name: Lille
major_races.context: After 24 years under the iconic socialist grande dame Martine Aubry, Lille enters the most open mayoral race of a generation. Aubry passed the baton to her first deputy Arnaud Deslandes, who was named mayor in 2025 but lacks Aubry's formidable political personality. Three serious challengers threaten the PS hold: Violette Spillebout (Macron's MP candidate), Stéphane Baly (the Greens candidate who came close in 2020) and Louis Delemer (LR). The city synonymous with the French left faces genuine uncertainty for the first time in a quarter century.

macron_endorsement.note: Spillebout is a sitting Renaissance MP and has direct Macron coalition backing with MoDem and Horizons on her list.

incumbent.name: Arnaud Deslandes
incumbent.note: Took over from Martine Aubry in 2025 after 24 years of Aubry's tenure.

candidates.name: Arnaud Deslandes
candidates.note: Aubry's chosen successor. First test without her behind him.

candidates.name: Violette Spillebout
candidates.note: Sitting MP, Renaissance + MoDem + Horizons. Second attempt after 2020.

candidates.name: Stéphane Baly
candidates.list: Lille, la ville que nous voulons
candidates.note: Near-winner in 2020.

candidates.name: Louis Delemer
candidates.note: LR + UDI. Supported by party president Bruno Retailleau.

candidates.name: Matthieu Valet
candidates.note: MEP.

polls.methodology: Online panel, n=800 registered voters, Lille
polls.note: Extremely tight potential runoff. The most open race in Lille in a generation. Baly's Greens are pivotal.

major_races.name: Rennes
major_races.context: The Breton capital is one of France's most educated and dynamic cities, with a large student and tech sector population. Reliably left-wing — Nathalie Appéré won with 65% in 2020. She seeks a third term in a more competitive environment. LFI has a strong presence with MP Marie Mesmeur as candidate, and the right runs under the Horizons-Renaissance ticket. With 11 lists competing, the first round could be unpredictable. Rennes remains likely left, but the margin will be much tighter than 2020.

macron_endorsement.note: A joint Horizons-Renaissance-MoDem list under 'Vivre Rennes!'.

candidates.name: Nathalie Appéré
candidates.list: Rennes, la ville que nous aimons
candidates.note: PS + EELV + PCF. 3rd term.

candidates.name: Marie Mesmeur
candidates.note: MP for Rennes 1st constituency.

candidates.name: Carole Gandon
candidates.note: Joint Horizons + Renaissance + MoDem. Two co-leaders.

candidates.name: Thomas Rousseau
candidates.note: LR secretary for Ille-et-Vilaine.

polls.methodology: Online panel, n=700 registered voters, Rennes

major_races.name: Grenoble
major_races.context: Home to Europe's largest science and technology cluster, Grenoble has been a laboratory for radical green and left politics under Éric Piolle (2014–2026 — two full terms). Piolle chose not to seek a third term. His successor is Laurence Ruffin, sister of the famous left-wing deputy François Ruffin. She represents a broad EELV-PS-PCF coalition. Her main challenger is the legendary Alain Carignon — LR mayor of Grenoble from 1983 to 1995, who was convicted of corruption and sentenced to prison in 1996 before returning to civic life. At 73, his comeback bid is one of the election's most remarkable stories.

macron_endorsement.note: Horizons backs Gerbi. Renaissance has no separate list. The centrist vote is small in this left-leaning university city.

incumbent.name: Éric Piolle
incumbent.note: Not running for a third term. Endorsed Laurence Ruffin.

candidates.name: Laurence Ruffin
candidates.note: Sister of deputy François Ruffin. EELV + PS + PCF + Génération.s. Piolle's chosen successor.

candidates.name: Alain Carignon
candidates.note: Former mayor (1983–95), convicted of corruption in 1996, returned to civic life. LR candidate at 73.

candidates.name: Allan Brunon
candidates.note: LFI candidate. Some local Insoumis dissidents actually backed Ruffin.

candidates.name: Hervé Gerbi
candidates.note: Horizons candidate, also backed by PRG and Place Publique.

polls.methodology: Online panel, n=700 registered voters, Grenoble
polls.note: Carignon's comeback is extraordinary but Ruffin is favoured with LFI fusion support.

departement_map.note: Département-level shading uses the party of the largest commune's current mayor as a proxy. Will update with actual results.
